quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern is a typical Wes Montgomery phrase that uses an Fmaj7 arpeggio over Dm7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the substitution principle — playing a major 7 arpeggio a minor third up over a minor 7 chord — attributed to a canonical bebop guitarist.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This is a typical altered scale run starting on the b13.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the entry point (b13) for the altered scale run, giving students a specific harmonic target from which to launch the phrase.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Pattern 77 starts with an 1235 pattern and encircles the 3rd.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines a foundational bebop motivic device — the 1235 cell combined with target-note encirclement — that recurs across chord types.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern can also be used on minor chords, such as in this example that uses Fmaj7 over Dm7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the major-chord pattern to minor contexts via the relative-major substitution, expanding the pattern's harmonic range.

quotes.topic: Repeating II–V pattern up a major second
quotes.verbatim_quote: Over a II VI VI progression, you can repeat the pattern a major second higher.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides the transposition rule for cycling the motif through a II–V–I–VI turnaround, a core bebop navigation technique.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Pattern 79 starts with a Dm7 arpeggio with a typical bebop rhythm.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Highlights that rhythmic placement is as definitive as pitch content in identifying bebop style.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This pattern can be used ove Cmaj7 or Am7 and features a chromatic descending line between the 7
and 5 of the major chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specifies the exact chromatic voice-leading target (7 down to 5) that gives Pattern 80 its characteristic colour over major and relative-minor chords.

quotes.verbatim_quote: You can also play this pattern with three pull-offs and a slide, like this:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces a legato articulation variant that changes the physical technique required for the same melodic line.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This pattern, that starts and ends on the 5th of a major chord, is very finger-friendly and easy to
play at higher tempos.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a practical performance rule — starting and ending on the 5th produces idiomatic fretting that facilitates speed.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can also play this pattern over dominant chords by changing the natural 7 to a b7 (the 5th note
in the pattern).
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the one-note modification rule that converts the major version of Pattern 81 into a dominant version.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
By changing the natural 3 of the dominant version to a b3 (second note in the pattern), it can be
played over minor chords as well.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Completes the three-chord-type derivation chain (major → dominant → minor) by identifying the single pitch change needed.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Pattern 82 uses tritone substitution by playing a Bb7 arpeggio over E7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explicitly names tritone substitution and maps it to a concrete arpeggio choice, making the harmonic mechanism actionable.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Pattern 83 starts with the chromatic part of the G (dominant) bebop scale and continues with a G7
arpeggio.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Demonstrates the standard bebop construction of leading into an arpeggio via the scale's chromatic passing tone.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can play a minor triad starting on the 3rd of a major chord.
For example: Em over Cmaj7
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the root-position rule for deploying minor triads over major chords — a reusable substitution formula.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can play a minor triad starting on the 5th of a minor chord.
For example: Am over Dm7
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives the parallel formula for minor-chord contexts, pairing with the major-chord rule to form a systematic substitution framework.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can play a minor triad starting on the 5th of a dominant chord.
For example: Dm over G7
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the minor-triad substitution system to dominant chords, completing the three core chord-type coverage.

quotes.topic: Minor triads over altered dominant (b9 and #9)
quotes.verbatim_quote:
You can play a minor triad starting on the b9 of a dominant chord.
For example: Abm over G7
quotes.why_load_bearing: Introduces the b9-rooted minor triad as an altered-dominant colour, expanding the substitution toolkit to tension-heavy situations.

quotes.topic: Connecting Am→Abm over II–V by half-step
quotes.verbatim_quote: This means that if you play an Am triad over Dm7, you can lower one fret and play Abm over G7.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Provides a practical fingerboard shortcut linking the Dm7 and G7 triad choices by a single fret shift.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Pattern 85 is the simple, but versatile movement of 9 to 1.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies the 9→1 resolution as a universally applicable motif, flagging its cross-chord-type usefulness.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Pattern 86 combines an Em7 arpeggio with a chromatic line.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the upper-structure arpeggio substitution (Em7 for G7) and notes that it is paired with chromatic passing tones.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This last pattern voice leads from 13 to b13 to the 9 of C major.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Articulates the specific chromatic voice-leading path through the II–V that makes Pattern 87 harmonically smooth.
